text: Raised bed gardens offer several advantages: better drainage, warmer soil in spring, reduced soil compaction, and easier weed control. Build beds 4 feet wide (to reach the center from either side) and 8-12 inches deep. Fill with a mix of 60% topsoil, 30% compost, and 10% perlite or vermiculite. Avoid using treated lumber for food gardens — use cedar, redwood, or untreated hardwood instead.

Companion planting improves garden productivity and pest management. The 'Three Sisters' method — corn, beans, and squash planted together — is a classic example. Corn provides a structure for beans to climb. Beans fix nitrogen in the soil. Squash leaves shade the ground, retaining moisture and suppressing weeds. Plant corn first, then beans when corn is 6 inches tall, then squash 1 week later.

Composting converts organic waste into nutrient-rich soil amendment. Maintain a carbon-to-nitrogen ratio of roughly 30:1. Carbon-rich ('brown') materials include dried leaves, straw, cardboard, and wood chips. Nitrogen-rich ('green') materials include food scraps, grass clippings, and manure. Turn the pile every 1-2 weeks to aerate. A well-managed hot compost pile reaches 130-160°F (54-71°C) and produces finished compost in 2-3 months.

Seed saving allows you to grow food independently of commercial seed suppliers. Start with open-pollinated varieties, not hybrids (F1). For tomatoes: let fruits fully ripen, scoop out seeds and pulp, ferment in water for 2-3 days until a mold layer forms, rinse clean seeds, and dry on a plate for 1-2 weeks. Store dried seeds in labeled envelopes in a cool, dry, dark location. Most vegetable seeds remain viable for 2-5 years when stored at 40°F and low humidity.

Soil testing should be done before planting. Contact your local Extension office for soil test kits or use a home test kit. Key measurements: pH (ideal range 6.0-7.0 for most vegetables), nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and potassium (K). To raise pH, add lime at 5-10 pounds per 100 square feet. To lower pH, add sulfur at 1-2 pounds per 100 square feet. Amend based on test results, not guesswork.

Succession planting extends the harvest season. Instead of planting all lettuce at once, plant a new row every 2-3 weeks. Fast-maturing crops like radishes (25 days), lettuce (45 days), and bush beans (50 days) are ideal for succession planting. In northern climates, start cool-season crops in early spring, warm-season crops after last frost, then cool-season again in late summer for a fall harvest.

Mulching conserves moisture, suppresses weeds, and regulates soil temperature. Apply 2-4 inches of organic mulch (straw, wood chips, shredded leaves) around plants after the soil has warmed in spring. Keep mulch 2-3 inches away from plant stems to prevent rot. As organic mulch decomposes, it adds nutrients to the soil. Replenish as needed throughout the growing season.
